00_Index/00_Project_Vision.md.content:
From the Hills and Foothills — Internal Vision
Why This Exists
This archive exists to systematically document the historical, economic, and cultural transformation of the Dooars and adjoining hills–foothill region.
The objective is long-term preservation, not short-term visibility.

Core Orientation
This is:

A research-driven archive
A structured documentation effort
A slow institutional build

This is NOT:

A social media growth project
A motivational platform
A political commentary space
A reactive content channel


Primary Focus (Initial 2–3 Years)
Geography:

Western Dooars / specific districts (refine progressively)

Time Focus:

Post-1865 British annexation
Plantation expansion period
Labour migration systems

Core Themes:

Tea plantation structure
Migration and settlement
Land & forest transformation
Demographic shifts
Cultural adaptation


My Role
I am functioning as:

Learner
Archivist
Systems thinker
Long-term documenter

Not as:

Public intellectual (yet)
Activist
Authority

Authority will be built through consistency.

Operating Principles

Facts and interpretation must be separated.
Every note must include:

Context
Key Points
Why It Matters
Questions Raised


No unverified claims.
Depth over speed.
Research before publication.


Long-Term Direction (10–15 Years)

Build a structured regional archive.
Develop interconnected knowledge maps.
Integrate data analysis where possible.
Enable future conversion into public reference website.
Maintain intellectual discipline.


Current Phase
Foundation-building.
Learning.
Mapping existing knowledge.
Identifying gaps.

00_Index/02_Dooars_Historical_Timeline.m.title: 02_Dooars_Historical_Timeline.md
00_Index/02_Dooars_Historical_Timeline.m.content:
Dooars Historical Timeline
This timeline outlines the major historical transitions that shaped the Dooars region, tracing its evolution from a Bhutan frontier zone to a colonial plantation landscape and later a modern administrative region.

Structural Phases of Dooars History

Bhutan Frontier Period (pre-1865)
British Annexation and Administration (1865–1900)
Plantation Economy Expansion (1870–1940)
Late Colonial Adjustments (1900–1947)
Post-Independence Transformation (after 1947)


Timeline of Key Events

Pre-Colonial Period – Frontier System
Before 1865 the Dooars functioned as a frontier zone between Bhutan and the plains of Bengal and Assam.
The region consisted of a series of mountain passes (Duars) that served as gateways between the Himalayan kingdom of Bhutan and the lowland territories.
These passes were administered by Bhutanese officials and were important for:

trade routes between Tibet, Bhutan, and the plains
frontier administration
military movement
tribute collection

Related Notes:
Dooars_Passes
Dooars_Pre_1865

1772 – Bhutan invasion of Cooch Behar
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse
Bhutanese forces invaded the kingdom of Cooch_Behar and captured its ruler.
The East India Company intervened militarily after the Cooch Behar authorities sought assistance.
This event marked the beginning of direct British political interaction with Bhutan.

1774 – Treaty between East India Company and Bhutan
1774_Treaty_with_Bhutan
Following the conflict over Cooch Behar, a treaty was concluded between the East India Company and Bhutan.
The agreement restored peace and opened diplomatic contact between British India and Bhutan.

1774–1775 – Bogle Mission to Tibet
1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet
Governor-General Warren_Hastings sent a diplomatic mission under George_Bogle to Tibet through Bhutan.
The mission aimed to establish trade relations between Bengal and Tibet and to explore commercial routes across the Himalayan frontier.
Although relations with the Tashi Lama were friendly, sustained trade did not develop.

1783 – Turner Mission to Tibet
1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet
A second British diplomatic mission led by Captain Samuel Turner travelled to Tibet through Bhutan.
The mission continued attempts to establish commercial links between Bengal and Tibet.

1791–1792 – Nepal–Tibet War
1791_Nepal_Tibet_War
Nepalese forces invaded Tibet, prompting military intervention by the Qing Empire of China.
After defeating Nepal, Chinese authorities strengthened control over Tibet and restricted foreign access to the region.
This effectively closed British attempts to establish trade with Tibet through Bhutan.

1792 – Bijni Dispute
1792_Bijni_Dispute
Conflict between Bhutan and the British Government regarding the succession of the Bijni ruler.
Early 19th Century – Frontier Disputes
Relations between Bhutan and British India were marked by frontier disputes involving:

territorial conflicts
raids along the frontier
disputes involving neighbouring zamindars and local rulers.

These tensions gradually increased British interest in the frontier region.

1815 – Mission of Kishen Kant Bose
1815_KishenKant_Bose_Mission
A diplomatic mission led by Kishen Kant Bose was sent by British authorities to Bhutan to address frontier disputes and gather information about the country.
His account provided one of the early descriptions of Bhutanese administration and society.

1864–1865 – Duar War
1864_Duar_War
The British Government of India fought a war against Bhutan over frontier disputes and political tensions in the Dooars region.
The conflict ended with Bhutan’s defeat.
Immediate Trigger:
Ashley_Eden_Mission_1864
1865 – Treaty of Sinchula
Treaty signed between British India and Bhutan after the Duar War.
Bhutan ceded the Bengal and Assam Dooars to British India.
Related Notes:
1864_Duar_War
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

Late 19th Century – Forest Administration and Plantation Development
Tea Plantation Expansion in Dooars
After annexation, the British administration reorganized the region through:

establishment of forest divisions
forest clearing
expansion of tea plantations.

The Dooars gradually developed into a major tea-producing region.

Late 19th – Early 20th Century – Labour Migration
Migration_of_Adivasi_Labour_to_Dooars
Large numbers of workers were recruited from regions such as:

Chota Nagpur plateau
Central India
present-day Jharkhand, Odisha, and Bihar.

These migrations led to the formation of plantation labour communities in the Dooars.

Early 20th Century – Consolidation of the Plantation Economy
The tea plantation economy became firmly established.
Transport infrastructure expanded and railway connections linked the Dooars to colonial trade networks.

1947 – Integration into Independent India
Following Indian independence in 1947, the Dooars became part of the Indian Union.
Administrative changes included:

integration into the state of West Bengal
district reorganizations
continuation of the plantation economy.


Observational Note
The history of the Dooars reflects a transition from:
Himalayan frontier zone
→ colonial annexation
→ environmental transformation
→ plantation economy
→ modern regional society.

01_Foundations/00_Foundations_Index.md.title: 00_Foundations_Index.md
01_Foundations/00_Foundations_Index.md.content:
Foundations – Structural Turning Points of the Dooars
This section outlines the major structural transformations that shaped the political, economic, and demographic evolution of the Dooars region.
Rather than presenting isolated facts, the archive treats the Dooars as a historical frontier space whose character changed through successive political and economic transitions.
These structural phases provide chronological and analytical continuity across all thematic sections of the archive.

I. Pre-Colonial Frontier Structure
During the pre-colonial period, the Dooars functioned primarily as a frontier zone between the Himalayan polities and the plains of Bengal and Assam.
Key characteristics included:

The Duars functioning as mountain passes connecting the plains with Bhutan
Political influence exercised by Bhutanese administrators (Penlows and Jungpens)
Tributary and political interactions with Cooch Behar and neighbouring states
Frontier trade networks linking Bhutan, Tibet, and the Bengal plains
The region acting as a buffer zone between hill polities and plains kingdoms

Related Notes:

Dooars_Geographical_Identity
Dooars_Pre_1865


II. Frontier Conflict and Territorial Transition (1864–1865)
This phase represents the major political rupture in the history of the Dooars.
Conflict between British India and Bhutan resulted in the Duar War (1864–1865), culminating in the Treaty of Sinchula.
This treaty transferred control of the Bengal and Assam Dooars from Bhutanese authority to the British colonial state.
Key consequences included:

End of Bhutanese administrative authority in the Dooars
Establishment of British frontier administration
Opening of the region to plantation capitalism and colonial land policy

Related Notes:

Duar War 1864–65
Treaty of Sinchula 1865


III. Colonial Consolidation (Post-1865)
Following annexation, the Dooars underwent rapid transformation under British colonial administration.
Major developments included:

Large-scale expansion of tea plantations
Introduction of colonial land settlement and revenue systems
Development of transport and administrative infrastructure
Implementation of forest regulation and extraction policies
Integration into the administrative framework of the Bengal Presidency

Related Notes:

Tea Plantation Expansion in Dooars
Colonial Forest Policy in Dooars


IV. Demographic Reconfiguration
The expansion of the plantation economy triggered major demographic changes across the region.
Key processes included:

Labour migration from Central and Eastern India
Establishment of tea garden settlements
Emergence of a multi-ethnic plantation society
Cultural and linguistic diversification across the Dooars

Related Notes:

Migration of Adivasi Labour to Dooars
Demographic_Profile_Dooars
Culture and Communities of Dooars


V. Late Colonial Adjustments (Early 20th Century)
During the late colonial period, frontier administration and Bhutan–British relations underwent further stabilization.
Important developments included:

The Treaty of Punakha (1910) and revised Bhutan–British relations
Stabilization of administrative boundaries in the frontier region
Consolidation of the plantation economy
Increasing integration of the Dooars into colonial economic networks

(Section to be expanded as research progresses)

VI. Post-Independence Reconfiguration (Post-1947)
Following Indian independence, the Dooars entered a new phase of administrative and economic transition.
Key processes included:

Integration into the Indian state structure
Administrative reorganization and district formation
Continuity of the plantation-based regional economy
Changing patterns of labour, migration, and rural settlement

(Future research section)

Observational Note
The Dooars should not be understood merely as a geographic region, but as a historical frontier landscape shaped by successive layers of political and economic transformation.
Across different periods the region has functioned as:

a Himalayan frontier zone
a colonial plantation belt
a post-independence administrative region

Each thematic section of the archive connects back to one or more of these structural phases.
This framework provides the analytical backbone for the entire “From the Hills and Foothills” archive.

01_Foundations/Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoo.title: Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoon.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoo.content:
Dooars Climate and Monsoon
Overview
The climate of the Dooars is strongly influenced by the eastern Himalayan foothills and the Indian monsoon system. The region receives heavy rainfall and maintains high humidity, which historically supported dense forests and later enabled the development of tea plantations.
Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_Forest_Landscape

Climatic Zone
The Dooars lies within a humid subtropical climatic zone influenced by the Himalayan mountain system.
Characteristics include:

heavy monsoon rainfall
warm and humid summers
mild winters
high atmospheric moisture

These climatic conditions support lush vegetation and rich biodiversity.

Monsoon Influence
The southwest monsoon plays a dominant role in shaping the climate of the Dooars.
During the monsoon months:

moist winds from the Bay of Bengal move northward
the Himalayan foothills force the air to rise
heavy rainfall occurs across the Dooars region

This process is known as orographic rainfall.

Rainfall Patterns
The Dooars receives some of the highest rainfall levels in eastern India.
Typical features include:

intense rainfall during the monsoon season
seasonal flooding of rivers
replenishment of river systems
fertile alluvial soil formation

These rainfall patterns strongly influence the region’s rivers and floodplains.
Related:
Dooars_River_Valleys

Environmental Effects
The monsoon climate historically supported:

dense forest ecosystems
extensive grasslands
abundant wildlife habitats
rich agricultural potential

Before colonial development, much of the region remained covered by forests.

Importance for Tea Cultivation
The climate of the Dooars later proved highly suitable for tea cultivation because it provides:

high rainfall
humid atmosphere
fertile soils
moderate temperatures

These conditions contributed to the expansion of tea plantations in the region during the late nineteenth century.

Historical Importance
Climate influenced the historical development of the Dooars by shaping:

forest distribution
settlement patterns
agricultural potential
plantation development during the colonial period.


Related Topics
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_River_Valleys
Dooars_Forest_Landscape

01_Foundations/Dooars_Ecological_Zones.m.title: Dooars_Ecological_Zones.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_Ecological_Zones.m.content:
Dooars Ecological Zones
Overview
The Dooars region forms part of the eastern Himalayan foothill ecosystem. Its landscape can be understood through a series of ecological zones that extend from the Bhutan hills down to the plains of northern Bengal and Assam.
These zones developed under the influence of climate, rivers, forests, and terrain.
Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_Forest_Landscape
Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoon

Himalayan Foothill Zone
The northern edge of the Dooars lies at the base of the Bhutan hills.
Characteristics:

rugged terrain near the foothills
numerous mountain streams descending from Bhutan
dense forests
wildlife habitats

This zone historically served as the frontier between Bhutan and the plains.
Related:
Eighteen_Dooars

Forest Belt
Much of the Dooars was historically covered by dense forests forming part of the eastern Himalayan forest ecosystem.
Features:

sal forests
riverine grasslands
rich biodiversity
elephant and rhinoceros habitats

These forests remained sparsely settled before the colonial period.
Related:
Dooars_Forest_Landscape

River Valley Zones
Between the major rivers of the region, fertile valleys developed.
These valleys formed natural corridors for:

movement
settlement
agriculture

Major river valleys include those formed by:

Teesta_River
Torsa_River
Jaldhaka_River
Raidak_River
Sankosh_River

Related:
Dooars_River_Valleys

Plantation and Settlement Zone
After the British annexation of the Dooars in 1865, large areas of forest were cleared to establish tea plantations.
Characteristics:

plantation estates
labour settlements
roads and transport networks
expanding market centres

This transformed the Dooars from a forest frontier into a plantation landscape.
Related:
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

Importance for Regional History
Understanding these ecological zones helps explain:

the early isolation of the region
patterns of settlement
the expansion of tea plantations
the environmental transformation of the Dooars during the colonial period.


Related Topics
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_River_Valleys
Dooars_Forest_Landscape
Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoon

01_Foundations/Dooars_Forest_Landscape.m.title: Dooars_Forest_Landscape.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_Forest_Landscape.m.content:
Dooars Forest Landscape
Overview
Before the expansion of tea plantations and colonial settlement, the Dooars region was largely covered by dense forests. These forests formed part of the eastern Himalayan foothill ecosystem and supported rich biodiversity.
The forest landscape played an important role in shaping the geography, settlement patterns, and economic development of the region.
Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_River_Valleys

Natural Environment
The forests of the Dooars developed under conditions of:

high monsoon rainfall
fertile alluvial soils
numerous rivers descending from the Bhutan hills
humid subtropical climate

These conditions created dense vegetation and diverse wildlife habitats.

Major Forest Types
Historically, the forests of the Dooars consisted mainly of:
Moist Deciduous Forests

dominated by sal (Shorea robusta)
mixed hardwood species
dense undergrowth

Riverine Forests

located along riverbanks
dominated by grasses, reeds, and flood-tolerant trees

Foothill Forests

found closer to the Bhutan hills
more rugged terrain with thick vegetation


Wildlife
The Dooars forests historically supported a wide range of wildlife, including:

elephants
rhinoceros
tigers
leopards
deer species

Many areas later became part of protected forests and wildlife reserves.

Human Interaction with Forests
Before large-scale colonial settlement, the forests were sparsely populated.
Local communities depended on the forests for:

hunting
forest produce
shifting cultivation

The dense forests also limited large-scale agriculture.

Colonial Transformation
After the British annexed the Dooars following the 1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula, large areas of forest were cleared for:

tea plantations
roads and settlements
timber extraction

This transformed the Dooars from a forest frontier into a plantation landscape.
Related:
Dooar_War_1864
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

Importance for Regional History
Understanding the forest landscape helps explain:

why the Dooars remained sparsely settled before colonial rule
how plantation agriculture expanded after forest clearing
the ecological transformation of the region.


Related Topics
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_River_Valleys

01_Foundations/Dooars_Identity.md.title: Dooars_Identity.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_Identity.md.content:
Dooars – Geographical Identity
Meaning of the Name

“Dooars” or “Duars” comes from the word “Dwar”, meaning door or gateway.
The term originally referred to the mountain passes connecting the Bengal and Assam plains with the Bhutan hills.
Over time the name came to describe the entire foothill region south of Bhutan where these passes opened into the plains.

Location

Foothill belt along the southern boundary of Bhutan.
Located between the Teesta River in the west and the Sankosh River in the east.
Primarily within modern Alipurduar and Jalpaiguri districts of West Bengal, with eastern extensions into Assam.
Part of the larger Terai–Dooars ecological region at the base of the eastern Himalayas.

Related places:

Buxa
Chamurchi
Dalimkote
Mynaguri
Cooch_Behar

Physical Features

Alluvial floodplains formed by rivers descending from the Himalayas.
Historically dense forests with rich biodiversity.
High rainfall zone influenced by Himalayan monsoon patterns.
Numerous seasonal and perennial rivers flowing southward from the hills.

Major rivers:

Teesta_River
Rungeet_River
Torsa
Jaldhaka
Raidak
Sankosh

Strategic Importance

Served as a frontier zone between Bhutan and the plains of Bengal and Assam.
Functioned historically as trade and movement corridors between the Himalayan region and the plains.
The passes were administered by Bhutanese officials known as Jungpens.
Became a military frontier during British expansion in the nineteenth century.

Related:

Dooar_War_1864
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

The Dooars Pass System
Historically the region contained eighteen mountain gateways known as the “Eighteen Dooars”, which connected Bhutan to the plains.
Important passes included:

Buxa
Chamurchi
Dalimkote
Mynaguri

Related:

Eighteen_Dooars

Settlement Patterns (Geography → Population Link)

River systems influenced early settlement clusters along fertile floodplains.
Dense forests limited large-scale settlement before colonial intervention.
Forest clearing during the tea plantation expansion created new labour settlements.
Plantation zones developed labour lines and estate-based communities.
Frontier geography encouraged migration from Nepal, Bhutan, and tribal regions of central India.

Why Geography Matters
The geography of the Dooars shaped its historical transformation.
It:

made the region suitable for tea cultivation
enabled colonial expansion after 1865
influenced the demographic structure of the region
transformed the Dooars into a major plantation frontier of eastern India

Related:

1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula
Demographic_Profile_Dooars
Dooars

01_Foundations/Dooars_River_System.md.title: Dooars_River_System.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_River_System.md.content:
Dooars River System
Overview
The geography of the Dooars is strongly shaped by a network of Himalayan rivers descending from the Bhutan hills into the plains of North Bengal and Assam.
These rivers create the fertile alluvial plains that define the Dooars landscape.
The river system has historically influenced:

settlement patterns
forest distribution
trade routes
tea plantation expansion

Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity

Major Rivers of the Dooars
The main rivers flowing through the Dooars region include:

Teesta_River
Torsa_River
Jaldhaka_River
Raidak_River
Sankosh_River

These rivers originate in the Himalayan and Bhutan hill systems and flow southward into the plains.

Geographic Structure of the Dooars
The Dooars region can be understood as a series of river valleys between major Himalayan rivers.
From west to east:

Teesta_River
Torsa_River
Jaldhaka_River
Raidak_River
Sankosh_River

These rivers divide the Dooars into multiple geographic zones.

Environmental Characteristics
The river system produces:

fertile alluvial floodplains
seasonal flooding
dense forest ecosystems
rich wildlife habitats

Historically these conditions limited early settlement but later supported tea plantation agriculture.

Historical Importance
The rivers played an important role in:

defining frontier boundaries
shaping transport routes
influencing location of early tea estates

Many early settlements and plantations developed along river valleys.

Related Topics
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Eighteen_Dooars
Dooars_Frontier_Pre_1865

01_Foundations/Dooars_River_Valleys.md.title: Dooars_River_Valleys.md
01_Foundations/Dooars_River_Valleys.md.content:
Dooars River Valleys
Overview
The geography of the Dooars is shaped by a series of river valleys formed by Himalayan rivers flowing southward from the Bhutan hills into the plains of northern West Bengal and Assam.
These rivers divide the Dooars into natural geographic belts. Each belt developed distinct settlement patterns, forest landscapes, and later plantation zones.
Related:
Dooars_River_System
Dooars_Geography_Identity

Major River Valleys of the Dooars
The Dooars landscape can be understood through the valleys formed between its major rivers.
Teesta–Torsa Valley
This region forms the western section of the Dooars.
Boundaries:

West: Teesta_River
East: Torsa_River

Characteristics:

western Dooars plains
early settlement zones
later tea plantation development


Torsa–Jaldhaka Valley
This forms part of the central Dooars.
Boundaries:

West: Torsa_River
East: Jaldhaka_River

Characteristics:

fertile floodplains
forest belts
plantation expansion during the colonial period


Jaldhaka–Raidak Valley
This valley forms an important section of the central and eastern Dooars.
Boundaries:

West: Jaldhaka_River
East: Raidak_River

Characteristics:

dense forests historically
later plantation zones
important wildlife habitats


Raidak–Sankosh Valley
This forms the eastern section of the Dooars.
Boundaries:

West: Raidak_River
East: Sankosh_River

Characteristics:

eastern Dooars plains
forest landscapes
frontier zones near Assam


Geographic Importance
These river valleys shaped the Dooars region by influencing:

forest distribution
settlement patterns
agricultural development
location of tea plantations

The river valleys also served as natural corridors for movement between the foothills and the plains.

Historical Importance
During the colonial period, many tea estates and settlements were established within these river valleys because of:

fertile soils
access to water
relatively flat terrain

The valleys therefore played a major role in the transformation of the Dooars into a plantation landscape.

Related Topics
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_River_System
Eighteen_Dooars

01_Foundations/Population_Transformation.title: Population_Transformation_Dooars.md
01_Foundations/Population_Transformation.content:
Population Transformation of the Dooars
Overview
The human history of the Dooars can be understood through three major stages of population transformation.
These stages reflect the transition of the region from a sparsely inhabited forest frontier into a plantation-based society during the colonial period.
Understanding this transformation helps explain the ethnic diversity, settlement patterns, and social structure of the modern Dooars.
Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity
Dooars_Forest_Landscape
Dooars_River_System

Stage 1 – Indigenous and Tribal Landscape
Before colonial expansion, the Dooars consisted largely of forests, river valleys, and scattered settlements.
Population density was low, and communities were adapted to the forest environment and the malarial climate of the region.
Characteristics:

shifting cultivation
forest-based subsistence
scattered riverine settlements
minimal urban development

Main communities included:

Mechi
Koch
Rabha
Lepcha
Bodo (in adjacent regions)

These communities depended on forests, rivers, and seasonal agriculture for their livelihood.
Related:
Precolonial_Settlement_Dooars
Dooars_Forest_Landscape

Stage 2 – Frontier Society
Before British annexation in 1865, the Dooars functioned as a frontier region between the Bhutan hills and the plains of Bengal.
The Bhutanese controlled the Duar passes and exercised authority over parts of the plains through frontier officials.
Characteristics:

frontier administration
tribute collection from plains
control of mountain passes
limited permanent settlement

Administrative authority was exercised by:

Bhotanese officials
frontier administrators such as Jungpens

Related:
Dooars_Frontier_Pre_1865
Eighteen_Dooars
Frontier_Society_Dooars

Stage 3 – Plantation Society
After the British annexation of the Dooars following the 1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula, the region underwent rapid economic transformation.
Large areas of forest were cleared to establish tea plantations, creating a new plantation-based economy.
This transformation led to large-scale migration of labour populations from different parts of India and Nepal.
Characteristics:

establishment of tea estates
plantation labour settlements (labour lines)
road and transport development
rapid demographic change

Major communities included:

Nepali
Adivasi labour communities from Chota_Nagpur
migrant workers from central and eastern India

Related:
Plantation_Society_Dooars
Migration_to_Dooars
Tea_Plantations_in_Dooars

Importance for Regional History
The population transformation of the Dooars explains:

the ethnic diversity of the region
the emergence of plantation labour communities
the transformation of forests into plantation landscapes
the development of modern settlements in the Dooars.

This framework is useful for understanding the historical development of specific locations and tea estates within the region.
Related:
Lankapara_Tea_Estate

02_History/British_Bhutan_Relations_1772.title: British_Bhutan_Relations_1772_1864.md
02_History/British_Bhutan_Relations_1772.content:
British–Bhutan Relations (1772–1864)
Overview
Relations between British India and Bhutan between 1772 and 1864 were characterized by intermittent diplomacy, frontier disputes, and failed attempts at establishing stable political and commercial relations.
These tensions gradually escalated and ultimately culminated in the Duar War (1864–1865).

Early Political Contact
British interaction with Bhutan began during the Bhutanese invasion of Cooch Behar in 1772.
The East India Company intervened militarily to restore the ruler of Cooch Behar, establishing the first direct political contact between the two states.
Related Note:
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse

Early Diplomatic Engagement
Following the conflict, diplomatic efforts were made to establish peaceful relations and expand trade.
Important developments included:

The 1774 treaty between Bhutan and the East India Company
The Bogle Mission to Tibet via Bhutan
The Turner Mission to Tibet

These missions aimed primarily at opening commercial routes between Bengal and Tibet.
Related Notes:
1774_Treaty_with_Bhutan
1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet
1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet

Closure of the Tibetan Trade Route
The Nepal–Tibet War (1791–1792) and subsequent Chinese intervention fundamentally altered regional geopolitics.
China strengthened its control over Tibet and restricted foreign access, effectively ending British hopes of establishing a trade route to Tibet through Bhutan.
Related Note:
1791_Nepal_Tibet_War

Frontier Disputes and Local Conflicts
Throughout the early nineteenth century, relations between Bhutan and British India were marked by recurring frontier disputes.
Key issues included:

Bhutanese influence over neighbouring frontier states such as Bijni
conflicts involving frontier zamindars
administrative ambiguity in the Dooars region

Related Notes:
1792_Bijni_Dispute
1815_KishenKant_Bose_Mission

Breakdown of Diplomatic Relations
Repeated diplomatic missions failed to produce a stable frontier settlement.
The situation worsened due to:

Bhutanese raids into frontier territories
disputes over jurisdiction in the Dooars
lack of centralized authority within Bhutan’s political structure

These tensions eventually led to the Ashley Eden mission to Bhutan in 1864, whose failure precipitated the Duar War.

Transition to Armed Conflict
The deterioration of diplomatic relations ultimately resulted in the Duar War (1864–1865).
The war ended with the Treaty of Sinchula, through which Bhutan ceded the Dooars to British India.
Related Notes:
1864_Duar_War
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

Historical Significance
The period from 1772 to 1864 represents the gradual transition from diplomatic engagement to military confrontation.
This transition reshaped the political geography of the eastern Himalayan frontier and led to the incorporation of the Dooars into the British colonial system.
Related Structural Note:
Dooars_Pre_1865

02_History/Frontier_Transition/00_Dooars.title: 00_Dooars_Frontier_System_Pre_1865.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/00_Dooars.content:
Dooars Frontier System (Before 1865)
Overview
Before 1865 the Dooars region functioned as a frontier zone between the plains of Bengal and the Himalayan kingdom of Bhutan. The region was controlled by Bhutan and administered through officials stationed at the Duar passes.
This frontier system shaped the political relations between Bhutan and the neighbouring plains territories.

Administrative Control
Bhutan administered the Dooars through frontier officials known as Jungpens.
These officials were responsible for:

supervising frontier territories
collecting tribute and taxes
regulating trade routes
maintaining Bhutanese authority in the Duars

Related:
Bhutan_Administration_of_Dooars

The Duar Passes
The term Duar means “door” or “gateway”.
These passes connected the plains of Bengal and Assam with the Bhutan hills and served as routes for:

trade
communication
military movement

Related:
Eighteen_Dooars
Dooars_Passes

Frontier Instability
The frontier region was often unstable. Disputes frequently occurred between Bhutanese authorities and neighbouring plains territories.
Examples include:

conflicts with Cooch Behar
disputes involving Bijni
repeated frontier raids

Related:
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse
1792_Bijni_Dispute

Diplomatic Attempts
The British Government attempted to resolve frontier disputes through diplomatic missions.
Important missions included:

1815_KishenKant_Bose_Mission
Ashley_Eden_Mission_1864

These efforts failed to produce a lasting settlement.

Collapse of the Frontier System
Continued tensions along the frontier eventually led to armed conflict between Bhutan and British India.
This conflict became the:
1864_Duar_War

Historical Importance
The frontier system that existed before 1865 explains the repeated disputes between Bhutan and the British.
The collapse of this system after the Duar War allowed the British to annex the Dooars and reorganize the region under colonial administration.
Related:
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1772_Brit.title: 1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1772_Brit.content:
1772 British–Bhutan Intercourse
Overview
The events of 1772 marked the beginning of direct interaction between the British East India Company and the Kingdom of Bhutan. The conflict arose when Bhutan intervened in the affairs of the neighbouring state of Cooch Behar.
British involvement in this dispute led to the first significant diplomatic and military engagement between the British and Bhutan.

Background
During the eighteenth century the Himalayan frontier was politically unstable.
The state of Cooch Behar was frequently involved in regional conflicts involving Bhutan and neighbouring powers.
In 1772 Bhutanese forces intervened in Cooch Behar and attempted to assert control over the territory.
The ruler of Cooch Behar sought assistance from the British East India Company.

British Intervention
In response to the appeal from Cooch Behar, the British East India Company agreed to provide military assistance.
British forces entered the region and confronted Bhutanese troops.
This intervention forced Bhutan to withdraw from Cooch Behar.

Diplomatic Consequences
Following the conflict, diplomatic negotiations were initiated between the British and Bhutan.
These negotiations eventually led to the dispatch of diplomatic missions to Bhutan and Tibet.
Related:
1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet

Historical Significance
The events of 1772 marked the beginning of British diplomatic engagement with Bhutan.
They also initiated a long history of frontier negotiations, disputes, and political interaction that eventually culminated in the:
1864_Duar_War

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1774_Bogl.title: 1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1774_Bogl.content:
1774–1775 – Bogle Mission to Tibet
Type
Event
Summary
Following the conclusion of peace between the East India Company and Bhutan in 1774, the British administration sought to establish diplomatic and commercial relations with Tibet.
The Governor-General of Bengal, Warren_Hastings, organized a diplomatic mission to Tibet. The mission was placed under the leadership of George_Bogle, a young civil servant of the East India Company.
The objective of the mission was to explore the possibility of establishing commercial relations between Bengal and Tibet, which the British believed could expand trade across the Himalayan frontier.

Journey Through Bhutan
The mission departed from Calcutta on 6 May 1774.
Bogle travelled through:

Cooch_Behar
the Bhutan frontier
Bhutanese administrative centres including Tassisudon (Tashichho Dzong)

He remained there for some time waiting for permission to proceed into Tibet.
Eventually the mission was allowed to continue northwards.

Arrival in Tibet
Bogle reached the court of the Tashi_Lama (Teshoo Lama) at Tashilhunpo Monastery in Tibet in October 1774.
The Tashi Lama received the British mission in a friendly manner.
Bogle stayed in Tibet until April 1775, developing cordial relations with the Tibetan authorities.

Objectives of the Mission
The mission sought to:

establish trade relations between Bengal and Tibet
improve diplomatic relations with Tibetan authorities
explore commercial routes through Bhutan and the Himalayan frontier

The British hoped that trade with Tibet might include goods such as:

wool
musk
gold dust
silk
horses
Himalayan products

In return, British and Indian manufactured goods could be exported northwards.

Outcome
Although the mission was diplomatically successful and friendly relations were established with the Tashi Lama, no long-term commercial treaty was finalized.
After the death of the Tashi Lama in 1780, the political situation in Tibet changed, and sustained trade relations with Bengal did not develop.
However, the mission laid the foundation for future diplomatic contact, including the 1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet.

Historical Significance
The Bogle Mission was important because it represented:

the first British diplomatic mission to Tibet
an attempt to establish Himalayan trade routes
an early stage of British strategic interest in the Bhutan–Tibet frontier.


Source
Bhutan_and_the_Dooar_War

Related People
George_Bogle
Warren_Hastings
Tashi_Lama

Related Places
Cooch_Behar
Bhutan
Tibet

Related Events
1774_Treaty_with_Bhutan
1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1774_Trea.title: 1774_Treaty_with_Bhutan.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1774_Trea.content:
1774 – Treaty between the East India Company and Bhutan
Type
Event
Summary
The Treaty of 1774 was concluded between the East India Company and the Bhutanese authorities following the conflict that began with the Bhutanese invasion of Cooch_Behar in 1772.
After British forces expelled Bhutanese troops from Cooch Behar, the Tashi Lama of Tibet intervened diplomatically and requested the British Government to cease hostilities against Bhutan.
The mediation led to negotiations between the two sides, resulting in a peace agreement in April 1774.

Key Terms of the Agreement
The treaty established peace between the two powers and included the following arrangements:

Restoration of peaceful relations between Bhutan and the East India Company
Bhutanese withdrawal from Cooch Behar
Re-establishment of diplomatic contact between Bhutan and British India

The agreement also created an opportunity for the British to pursue commercial relations with Tibet through Bhutan.

Diplomatic Consequences
The favourable tone of communications from the Tashi Lama made a strong impression on the British administration in Bengal.
As a result, Governor-General Warren_Hastings decided to send a diplomatic mission to Tibet to explore the possibility of trade relations.
This led directly to the 1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet.

Historical Significance
The Treaty of 1774 was significant because it:

ended the immediate conflict between Bhutan and the East India Company
established formal diplomatic communication between the two governments
opened the way for British diplomatic missions to Tibet
marked the beginning of British strategic interest in the Himalayan frontier.


Source
Bhutan_and_the_Dooar_War

Related Places
Cooch_Behar
Bhutan
Tibet

Related People
Warren_Hastings
Tashi_Lama

Related Events
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse
1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1783_Turn.title: 1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1783_Turn.content:
1783 – Turner Mission to Tibet
Type
Event
Summary
In 1783, the British Government of Bengal organized a second diplomatic mission to Tibet following the earlier mission of George_Bogle.
The mission was led by Captain Samuel Turner, an officer of the Bengal Army. Its primary purpose was to maintain diplomatic relations with Tibetan authorities and to continue British attempts to establish commercial connections with Tibet through the Himalayan frontier.
The mission was also intended to convey the congratulations of the British Government on the recognition of the new incarnation of the Tashi Lama, who had died in 1780.

Journey and Route
The mission departed from Calcutta and travelled north through the plains of Bengal.
The route included:

Murshidabad
Rangpur
Cooch_Behar

From the frontier town of Chichacotta in the Dooars region, the mission entered Bhutan.
The party travelled through Buxa Dooar, following a route similar to that taken earlier by the 1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet.

Delay in Bhutan
Upon reaching Tassisudon (Tashichho Dzong), the mission was delayed for several months while awaiting permission from the Tibetan authorities to proceed further north.
Eventually approval was granted, though with a restriction that only two members of the mission could continue into Tibet.

Arrival in Tibet
Captain Turner and his companions eventually reached Tashilhunpo, the seat of the Tashi Lama.
Turner reported that the Tibetan authorities appeared friendly toward the idea of commercial relations with Bengal. However, he also observed that Tibetan officials were cautious due to the influence of Chinese authority in the region.

Outcome
Although the mission strengthened diplomatic relations, no lasting commercial agreement was achieved.
Later political developments, particularly the Nepal–Tibet War (1791–1792) and increased Chinese control over Tibet, prevented further British attempts to establish trade routes through Bhutan.

Historical Significance
The Turner Mission was important because it:

continued British diplomatic engagement with Tibet
confirmed British strategic interest in the Himalayan frontier
provided valuable information about the political situation in Tibet and Bhutan.

However, it also demonstrated the limitations of British influence in the region.

Source
Bhutan_and_the_Dooar_War

Related People
Samuel_Turner
Warren_Hastings
George_Bogle

Related Places
Cooch_Behar
Buxa_Dooar

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1791_Nepa.title: 1791_Nepal_Tibet_War.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1791_Nepa.content:
1791–1792 – Nepal–Tibet War
Type
Event
Summary
In 1791, forces from the Kingdom of Nepal (the Gorkha state) invaded Tibet. The invasion was reportedly encouraged by a refugee lama who exaggerated the wealth of Tibetan monasteries, particularly the monastery of the Tashi Lama at Tashilhunpo.
The Nepalese army attacked Tibetan territory and plundered the monastery of Tashilhunpo, an important religious centre.
This conflict significantly altered the political situation in the Himalayan region.

Chinese Intervention
The invasion of Tibet triggered a response from the Qing Empire of China, which claimed authority over Tibet.
The Chinese Emperor Qianlong dispatched a large military force to assist Tibet.
Chinese forces defeated the Nepalese army and advanced into Nepalese territory, eventually reaching close to Kathmandu.
Facing defeat, the Nepalese ruler accepted Chinese terms and acknowledged Chinese authority.

Consequences
After the war:

China strengthened its political control over Tibet
military posts were established along the Tibetan frontier
foreign access to Tibet became more restricted

These developments effectively closed Tibet to direct diplomatic and commercial contact with British India.

Impact on British Plans
Before this conflict, British officials had attempted to establish trade with Tibet through Bhutan via the missions of:

1774_Bogle_Mission_to_Tibet
1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet

However, after the Chinese consolidation of power in Tibet, the possibility of sustained trade between Bengal and Tibet through Bhutan largely disappeared.

Historical Significance
The Nepal–Tibet War marked a turning point in Himalayan geopolitics.
It resulted in:

stronger Chinese control over Tibet
the closing of Tibet to British diplomatic and commercial initiatives
a shift in British frontier policy in the Himalayan region.


Source
Bhutan_and_the_Dooar_War

Related Places
Nepal
Tibet
Tashilhunpo

Related Events

1783_Turner_Mission_to_Tibet

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1792_Bijn.title: 1792_Bijni_Dispute.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1792_Bijn.content:
1792 Bijni Dispute
Overview
The Bijni dispute was a frontier conflict involving the Bhutanese authorities and the state of Bijni in the late eighteenth century. The dispute reflected the unstable political conditions that existed along the Dooars frontier.
The conflict also attracted the attention of the British East India Company, which was increasingly involved in regional affairs.

Background
Bijni was a small frontier state located near the foothills of the eastern Himalayas.
Bhutan attempted to assert authority over certain territories associated with Bijni and the surrounding frontier areas.
These actions created disputes regarding tribute, jurisdiction, and territorial control.

Nature of the Conflict
The dispute involved:

Bhutanese attempts to control frontier territories
disagreements over tribute and taxation
conflicts between Bhutanese officials and local authorities

The instability of the frontier made the situation difficult to resolve.

British Interest
The British East India Company monitored these developments closely because they affected the stability of the frontier.
Conflicts such as the Bijni dispute contributed to British concerns about Bhutanese influence in the plains.

Historical Significance
The Bijni dispute illustrates the fragile political balance that existed in the Dooars region before British annexation.
Repeated conflicts of this kind eventually contributed to growing tensions between Bhutan and British India.
These tensions ultimately formed part of the background to the:
1864_Duar_War

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1800s_Bhu.title: Dooars
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1800s_Bhu.content:
Dooars
Meaning of the Name
The word Dooars (Duars) comes from the Hindustani word “Dwar”, meaning door or gateway.
These “doors” refer to the mountain passes connecting the plains of India with Bhutan and further north with Tibet.
Originally the term referred to the passes themselves, but later it came to refer to the fertile plains at the foot of the Bhutan hills.
Location
The Dooars form a narrow belt along the foothills of the Eastern Himalayas separating the plains of Bengal and Assam from Bhutan.
Extent
The region extends roughly:

Length: about 220 miles
Width: between 10 and 30 miles

Landscape
Northern Zone

Rugged terrain
Spurs descending from the Himalayan mountains
Dense forests

Wildlife historically included elephants, rhinoceros, deer, tigers, buffalo and large snakes.
Southern Zone
The southern part consists of fertile soil capable of growing crops such as:

rice
cotton
tobacco

Tobacco from the Dooars was an important supply for Bhutan.
Climate
The climate of the Dooars was historically considered unhealthy because of dense jungle and malaria. Many people could not remain there for long periods without illness.
Historical Importance
The Dooars controlled the routes between the plains of India and the kingdom of Bhutan. Because of their fertile land and strategic location they became a major source of conflict between Bhutan and British India, eventually leading to the Dooar War (1864–1865).
See Also
Dooars Identity
Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War, Chapter 3

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1815_Kish.title: 1815_KishenKant_Bose_Mission.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1815_Kish.content:
1815 Kishen Kant Bose Mission
Overview
The mission of Kishen Kant Bose to Bhutan in 1815 was an attempt by the British Government to negotiate a settlement of frontier disputes between Bhutan and British India.
The mission reflected continuing efforts to stabilize relations along the Dooars frontier.

Background
During the early nineteenth century the frontier between Bhutan and the British-controlled plains remained unstable.
Disputes continued over:

raids into frontier territories
tribute claims
jurisdiction in the Dooars region

These problems led the British Government to pursue diplomatic negotiations.

The Mission
Kishen Kant Bose was sent as an emissary to Bhutan with the aim of discussing frontier disputes and establishing improved relations between the two governments.
The mission sought to:

address frontier tensions
negotiate settlements regarding territorial disputes
improve diplomatic communication between Bhutan and British India.


Outcome
Although the mission represented a diplomatic effort to resolve conflicts, it did not produce a lasting settlement of frontier disputes.
Problems along the Dooars frontier continued during the following decades.

Historical Significance
The mission illustrates the continued attempts by British authorities to resolve frontier disputes through diplomacy.
However, repeated failures of diplomatic efforts eventually contributed to the escalation of tensions that culminated in the:
1864_Duar_War

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1828_Anne.title: 1828 Annexation of Assam and Bhutan Frontier Relations
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1828_Anne.content:
1828 Annexation of Assam and Bhutan Frontier Relations
Context
For several years after the mission of Kishen Kant Bose in 1815, there was little recorded communication between the British Government and Bhutan.
The situation changed after the First Burmese War when the British expelled the Burmese from Assam and took control of the region.
What Happened
In 1828, the British Government assumed control of Lower Assam.
By doing so, the British also inherited the political relations and disputes that previously existed between the Assamese rulers and neighbouring hill states, especially Bhutan.
Frontier Situation
During the earlier period of political instability in Assam:

Border tribes frequently descended from the hills
They occupied land in the plains
The Assamese rulers were unable to stop them

Among these tribes, the Bhutanese benefited the most, gaining control over several tracts of land in the plains known as the Dooars.
Importance of the Dooars
The Dooars were fertile plains located at the foothills of the Himalayas.
Control of these territories became the main reason for the gradual involvement of the British Government with Bhutan.
Historical Significance
The British annexation of Assam in 1828 fundamentally changed the political dynamics of the Bhutan frontier.
From this point onward, disputes over the Dooars and frontier raids increasingly strained relations between the British and Bhutan governments.
These tensions eventually contributed to the Dooar War (1864–1865).
Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War, Chapter 3

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1839_Bhut.title: Bhutanese Frontier Raids and Abductions
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1839_Bhut.content:
Bhutanese Frontier Raids and Abductions (1839)
Context
After the British annexed Lower Assam in 1828, they inherited the unstable frontier relations that had previously existed between the Assamese rulers and the Bhutanese authorities.
The Dooars region remained under Bhutanese influence, and the frontier between the plains and the Bhutan hills was difficult to control.
The Incident
During 1839, a number of Bhutanese raids took place across the frontier into British territory.
Twelve people from British-controlled territory were abducted by Bhutanese raiders.
The fate of these captives was severe:

one prisoner died from wounds
another was killed while trying to escape
a third was injured and thrown down a precipice for refusing to work

These acts created serious tension along the frontier.
British Response
The British authorities viewed these repeated raids and abductions as a threat to the security of the frontier population.
To prevent further aggression, the Government of India decided to take administrative control of the Assam Dooars.
In 1841, the British government formally took over the management of these territories while agreeing to pay Bhutan an annual compensation of 10,000 rupees.
Consequences
This arrangement reduced raids into British territory but did not completely end frontier disturbances.
Incursions from the Bhutan hills into the villages of the Dooars continued for many years.
Historical Significance
The frontier raids of 1839 marked a turning point in relations between Bhutan and British India.
They demonstrated:

the difficulty of maintaining security along the Himalayan frontier
the weakness of Bhutanese administrative control in the plains
the increasing involvement of the British government in the affairs of the Dooars

These developments eventually contributed to the Dooar War (1864–1865).
Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War, Chapter 3

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1842_Amba.title: Ambaree Fallacottah Dispute
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1842_Amba.content:
title: Ambaree Fallacottah Dispute
type: event
year: 1842

Falakata

1828_Annexation_of_Assam_and_Bhutan_Frontier.md




Ambaree Fallacottah Dispute
Context
Ambaree Fallacottah was a large estate situated in the plains of the Bengal Dooars. Although the land lay within the British frontier, it had earlier been taken from the Zemindar of Bykantpore and handed over to the Bhutanese authorities in 1784.
Because the estate was located inside British territory, Bhutanese officials faced considerable difficulty administering it.
Conflict
The estate gradually became a source of unrest on the frontier.
A member of the Bykantpore family attempted to regain control of the land by refusing to pay rent to the Bhutanese authorities. He was also accused of conducting raids into Bhutan and hiding plundered goods within the estate.
As a result, the estate became a kind of lawless frontier zone, where effective control by either Bhutan or the British authorities was difficult.
British Intervention
The Bhutanese government eventually acknowledged that it could not manage the estate effectively because it was located far inside British territory.
Therefore, Bhutan requested the British government to administer the estate and collect its revenue.
The British authorities agreed to this arrangement.
In 1842, the estate of Ambaree Fallacottah was placed under British management, with the understanding that the net annual revenue from the property would continue to be paid to Bhutan.
Historical Significance
The Ambaree Fallacottah dispute reveals the complicated political situation along the Dooars frontier during the early nineteenth century.
It illustrates:

Bhutan’s difficulty in controlling territories located in the plains
the gradual extension of British administrative influence in the Dooars
increasing frontier tensions between Bhutan and British India

These growing tensions eventually contributed to the annexation of the Dooars following the Dooar War (1864–1865).
Connection to Present-day Geography
The historical estate of Ambaree Fallacottah is closely associated with the region around present-day Falakata in Alipurduar district, West Bengal.
Over time, the name Fallacottah evolved into Falakata, which today is an important town in the eastern Dooars.
This connection makes the Ambaree Fallacottah dispute an important early reference to the historical development of the Falakata region.
Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War, Chapter 3

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1864_Ashl.title: 1864_Ashley_Eden_Mission.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1864_Ashl.content:
Ashley Eden Mission (1864)
Overview
The Ashley Eden Mission of 1864 was a diplomatic mission sent by the British Government of India to Bhutan in an attempt to resolve longstanding frontier disputes.
The failure of this mission became the immediate cause of the:
1864_Duar_War

Background
Relations between Bhutan and British India had deteriorated due to recurring frontier problems.
These included:

Bhutanese raids into the plains
disputes over tribute and taxation
conflicts involving frontier states such as Bijni
boundary disagreements in the Dooars region

The British Government hoped that a diplomatic mission might resolve these issues.

The Mission
Ashley Eden was appointed as the British envoy and sent to Bhutan to negotiate a settlement with the Bhutanese authorities.
However, the mission encountered hostility and difficult conditions during its negotiations.
According to British accounts, Eden was pressured into signing an unfavourable agreement.

Consequences
The British Government regarded the treatment of the mission as unacceptable.
The failure of the negotiations convinced British authorities that diplomacy had failed.
Shortly afterwards the British Government launched the military campaign known as the:
1864_Duar_War

Historical Significance
The Ashley Eden Mission represents the final diplomatic attempt to resolve frontier disputes between Bhutan and British India.
Its failure directly led to the outbreak of war and the subsequent annexation of the Dooars by the British.

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1864_Dooa.title: 1864_Dooar_War.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1864_Dooa.content:
1864 Duar War
Overview
The Duar War (1864–1865) was a military conflict between the British Government of India and the Kingdom of Bhutan. The war resulted in the annexation of the Bengal and Assam Dooars by British India.
This conflict represents a major turning point in the political history of the Dooars.

Background
Throughout the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries, relations between Bhutan and the British Government were marked by disputes along the frontier.
Key problems included:

Bhutanese raids into plains territories.
Disputes with local zamindars.
Conflicts over the control of frontier states such as Bijni.
Ongoing boundary disputes in the Dooars region.

Earlier diplomatic attempts to resolve these tensions were unsuccessful.
Related Notes:
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse
1792_Bijni_Dispute
1815_KishenKant_Bose_Mission

Immediate Cause
The immediate cause of the war was the failure of the British diplomatic mission led by:
Ashley_Eden_Mission_1864
The mission was sent to negotiate frontier disputes but ended unsuccessfully and increased tensions between the two governments.

Military Campaign
In November 1864 British forces launched military operations against Bhutan.
The campaign involved advances through several Dooar passes including:

Buxa Dooar
Dalingkot (Dalimkot)

British troops gradually captured Bhutanese forts along the frontier.

End of the War
The conflict ended with the signing of:
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula
Under this treaty Bhutan ceded the Dooars to British India.

Historical Significance
The Duar War transformed the political geography of the region.
Major outcomes included:

British control of the Dooars.
Establishment of colonial administration.
Opening of the region for plantation development.

The war marks the beginning of the colonial transformation of the Dooars.

Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War

02_History/Frontier_Transition/1865_Trea.title: 1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula.md
02_History/Frontier_Transition/1865_Trea.content:
type: treaty
year: 1865
region: dooars
topic: colonial_transition
actors: british_india, bhutan
period: colonial
1865 Treaty of Sinchula
Overview
The Treaty of Sinchula was signed in 1865 between the British Government of India and the Kingdom of Bhutan following the Duar War. The treaty formally ended the conflict and established new territorial arrangements between the two states.
Under this agreement Bhutan ceded the Bengal and Assam Dooars to British India, marking the beginning of British administration in the region.

Background
Relations between Bhutan and British India had been unstable for decades due to disputes along the frontier.
Several issues contributed to tensions:

Bhutanese raids into the plains territories.
Conflicts with neighbouring frontier states such as Cooch Behar and Bijni.
Disputes over tribute and jurisdiction in the Dooars.
Failed diplomatic negotiations between the two governments.

These tensions eventually led to the outbreak of the:
1864_Duar_War

Terms of the Treaty
The Treaty of Sinchula established several important conditions.
Territorial Changes
Bhutan ceded:

the Bengal Dooars
the Assam Dooars
the hill territory of Dewangiri

to the British Government of India.
Annual Subsidy
In return for these territorial concessions, the British Government agreed to provide Bhutan with an annual subsidy.

Consequences
The treaty had major consequences for the region.

The Dooars came under British colonial administration.
The frontier between Bhutan and British India was reorganized.
Large areas of forest were opened for economic development.
The region was later developed for tea plantation expansion.


Historical Significance
The Treaty of Sinchula represents a major turning point in the history of the Dooars.
It marks the transition of the region from:
Bhutanese frontier territory → British colonial administration.
This transformation later led to the development of tea plantations and new settlement patterns.

Dooars_Transformation_Timeline_1864_1900

Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War

02_History/Tea_Plantations_in_Dooars.md.title: Tea_Plantations_in_Dooars.md
02_History/Tea_Plantations_in_Dooars.md.content:
Tea Plantations in the Dooars
type: economic_system
period: colonial
region: dooars
topic: plantation
Overview
The Dooars region became one of the major tea-producing areas of India during the late nineteenth century.
Following the British annexation of the Dooars after the 1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula, large areas of forest were cleared to establish tea plantations.
These plantations transformed the region economically, socially, and environmentally.
Related:
Plantation_Society_Dooars
Migration_to_Dooars
Dooars_Forest_Landscape

Early Development
Tea cultivation in the Dooars began in the late nineteenth century as European planters expanded plantation agriculture from the Darjeeling hills into the foothill plains.
The fertile alluvial soil, heavy rainfall, and humid climate of the region made it highly suitable for tea cultivation.
Related:
Dooars_Climate_and_Monsoon
Dooars_River_Valleys

Plantation Expansion
During the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, numerous tea estates were established across the Dooars.
Large tracts of forest were cleared and converted into plantation estates.
Plantation development included:

establishment of tea gardens
construction of labour lines
development of transport routes
creation of plantation settlements

Related:
Labour_Lines_Tea_Estates

Labour and Migration
The tea plantations depended on a large labour force.
Workers were recruited from different regions including:

Nepali migrants
Adivasi communities from Chota_Nagpur
other labour groups from central and eastern India

These communities formed the core population of plantation settlements.
Related:
Migration_to_Dooars

Plantation Landscape
The expansion of tea estates transformed the landscape of the Dooars.
Changes included:

clearing of dense forests
development of plantation fields
emergence of plantation villages
growth of market towns near tea estates

This transformation shaped the modern geography and economy of the region.

Tea Estates of the Dooars
Numerous tea estates were established across the Dooars during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.
Some important estates include:

Lankapara_Tea_Estate
Kalchini_Tea_Estate

Bagrakote_Tea_Estate

This list can be expanded as more estates are documented.

Importance for Regional History
The development of tea plantations explains:

the economic transformation of the Dooars
large-scale migration into the region
the emergence of plantation labour communities
the development of modern settlements in the Dooars.

06_Places/Cooch_Behar.md.content:
Dooars Passes (Duars)
Definition
The term Dooars (Duars) derives from the Hindustani word “door” or “gateway.”
Historically, the Dooars referred to the mountain passes connecting the plains of Bengal and Assam with the kingdom of Bhutan.
These passes served as:

frontier gateways into Bhutan
trade routes between Tibet, Bhutan, and the plains
military entry points
administrative frontier zones

Over time, especially under British rule after 1865, the term “Dooars” came to refer to the entire foothill plains region south of Bhutan.

Administrative Context (Pre-1865)
Before British annexation, the Dooars were controlled by Bhutanese authorities.
The passes were administered through local officials known as:

Penlows – provincial governors
Jungpens – fort governors controlling specific passes and frontier districts

These officials managed trade, tribute collection, and military control of the frontier.

Bengal (Western) Dooars
The Bengal frontier traditionally consisted of eleven Dooar passes connecting Bhutan with the plains of present-day Jalpaiguri and Alipurduar districts.
Commonly recorded passes include:

Buxa Dooar
Chamurchi Dooar
Dalimkot Dooar
Joomerkote Dooar
Bagrakote Dooar
Lakshmi (Laxmi) Dooar
Bhalka / Bhulka Dooar
Daling Dooar
Tendu Dooar
Jainti (Jainty) Dooar
Kumargram Dooar

Evidence Status
This list appears in colonial administrative records and gazetteers, though spellings and classifications vary between sources.
Further verification through historical maps and gazetteers is recommended.

Assam (Eastern) Dooars
The Bhutan frontier with Assam consisted of seven principal Dooar passes.
Commonly listed Assam Dooars include:

Bijni Dooar
Sidli Dooar
Chirang Dooar
Ripu Dooar
Chapaguri Dooar
Banska Dooar
Darranga Dooar

These connected Bhutan with the plains of Goalpara and Kamrup regions.

Historical Transformation (1865)
The Dooars region changed fundamentally after the Duar War (1864–1865).
Following the Treaty of Sinchula (1865):

Bhutan ceded the Bengal and Assam Dooars to the British
the region was incorporated into British India
forests were gradually cleared
tea plantations expanded across the foothill plains

This marked the beginning of the plantation economy of the Dooars.

Relationship to Modern Dooars Region
Today the term “Dooars” refers to the entire foothill belt south of Bhutan, particularly across:

Alipurduar district
Jalpaiguri district
parts of Darjeeling district

Many modern settlements and tea estates developed in areas historically associated with these frontier gateways.

Research Note
The precise geographical correspondence between some historical Dooar passes and modern locations remains uncertain.
Further investigation using:

colonial frontier maps
district gazetteers
British survey records

may clarify the exact locations of certain passes.

Related Notes
Dooars_Pre_1865
1772_British_Bhutan_Intercourse
1864_Duar_War
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula

06_Places/Dooars.md.title: Dooars
06_Places/Dooars.md.content:
Dooars
Meaning of the Name
The word Dooars (Duars) comes from the Hindustani word “Dwar”, meaning door or gateway.
These “doors” refer to the mountain passes that connect the plains of India with the Himalayan kingdom of Bhutan and further north to Tibet.
Originally the term referred to the passes themselves, but over time it came to describe the entire fertile belt of land at the foot of the Bhutan hills.
Location
The Dooars form a long narrow strip of land along the foothills of the Eastern Himalayas.
They separate:

the plains of Assam and Bengal
from the hill kingdom of Bhutan

Extent
Approximate dimensions of the region:

Length: about 220 miles
Width: between 10 and 30 miles

The region stretches along the base of the Bhutan hills and forms an important frontier zone between the mountains and the plains.
Landscape
Northern Zone
The northern portion of the Dooars lies close to the hills and consists of:

uneven and rugged terrain
spurs descending from the Himalayan mountains
dense forests and thick vegetation

Wildlife historically found in these forests included:

elephants
rhinoceros
tigers
deer
buffalo
large snakes

Southern Zone
The southern part of the Dooars consists of fertile alluvial soil.
Agriculture was possible here and crops grown included:

rice
cotton
tobacco

The region was one of the main sources of tobacco for Bhutan.
Climate
Historically the Dooars were known for an unhealthy climate, particularly in forested areas.
Conditions included:

heavy jungle vegetation
malaria and other diseases
high mortality among people stationed there for long periods

Because of this, soldiers and administrators often found it difficult to remain in the region.
Historical Importance
The Dooars were extremely important because they:

controlled access routes between Bhutan and the plains
contained fertile agricultural land
served as an economic resource for Bhutan
became a strategic frontier region between British India and Bhutan

Control of the Dooars eventually became the main cause of conflict between the British and Bhutan, leading to the Dooar War (1864–1865).
See Also
Dooars Identity
Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War, Chapter 3

The Raidak flows southward from Bhutan into the plains of the Dooars and eventually joins the Brahmaputra river system.

Within the Dooars, the Raidak flows through:

Alipurduar region

Monsoon-driven flooding
shifting river channels
rich alluvial deposits

The Sankosh River is one of the largest rivers flowing through the eastern Dooars and forms an important geographic boundary in the region.

Course
The Sankosh flows southward from Bhutan into the plains of the Dooars and continues into Assam before joining the Brahmaputra river system.
Dooars Regions Influenced
Within the Dooars, the Sankosh flows through:

eastern Alipurduar region
Kumargram area
the Bhutan–Assam frontier zone

Geographic Importance
The Sankosh River historically marks an important regional boundary.
It forms the eastern limit of the Bengal Dooars and separates them from the Assam Dooars.
Environmental Characteristics

strong seasonal flow during monsoon
formation of extensive floodplains
rich forest and wildlife habitats

The river historically functioned as:

a frontier boundary
a natural division between administrative regions of the Dooars.

The Teesta River is one of the major Himalayan rivers of eastern India and forms the western boundary of the Dooars region.

The Teesta flows through:

Darjeeling hills

10_Concepts/Frontier-System/Bhutan_Admin.title: Bhutan_Administration_of_Dooars.md
10_Concepts/Frontier-System/Bhutan_Admin.content:
type: system
region: dooars
topic: frontier_governance
actors: bhutan
period: pre_1865
Bhutan Administration of the Dooars
Overview
Before 1865 the Dooars region formed the southern frontier of the Kingdom of Bhutan. The territory was administered through Bhutanese frontier officials who controlled the important passes connecting the hills and the plains.
The region served as both a defensive frontier and a source of revenue for Bhutan.

The Meaning of Duars
The word Duar means “door” or “gateway”.
These gateways connected the plains of Bengal and Assam with the Bhutan hills.
Control over these passes allowed Bhutan to regulate trade and communication between the mountains and the plains.
Related:
Dooars_Passes
Eighteen_Dooars

Frontier Officials
The Bhutanese administration of the Dooars was carried out by officials known as Jungpens.
Their responsibilities included:

administering frontier territories
collecting tribute
supervising trade routes
maintaining authority over the passes


Revenue System
The Dooars were an important economic zone for Bhutan.
Revenue sources included:

tribute from plains territories
agricultural taxes
duties on trade passing through the frontier routes


Frontier Instability
The frontier region was often unstable due to:

disputes with plains states
raids across the frontier
conflicts over tribute and authority

These tensions gradually intensified during the nineteenth century.

British Intervention
As British power expanded in northern Bengal and Assam, disputes with Bhutan increased.
British authorities criticized the Bhutanese administration of the frontier and accused Bhutan officials of raids and misrule.
These tensions eventually contributed to the outbreak of the:
1864_Duar_War

Historical Significance
Understanding the Bhutanese administration of the Dooars helps explain the political situation of the region before British annexation.
The collapse of this frontier system after 1865 allowed the British to reorganize the region and later develop tea plantations.
Related:
1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula
Tea_Plantations_in_Dooars

Source
Bhutan and the Dooar War

Joomerkote Dooar
Bagrakote Dooar
Lakshmi (Laxmi) Dooar
Bhalka / Bhulka Dooar
Daling Dooar
Tendu Dooar
Jainti (Jainty) Dooar
Kumargram Dooar

Evidence Status
This list appears in colonial administrative records and gazetteers, though spellings and classifications vary between sources.
Further verification through historical maps and gazetteers is recommended.

Assam (Eastern) Dooars
The Bhutan frontier with Assam consisted of seven principal Dooar passes.
Commonly listed Assam Dooars include:

Bijni Dooar
Sidli Dooar
Chirang Dooar
Ripu Dooar
Chapaguri Dooar
Banska Dooar
Darranga Dooar

These connected Bhutan with the plains of Goalpara and Kamrup regions.

Historical Transformation (1865)
The Dooars region changed fundamentally after the Duar War (1864–1865).
Following the Treaty of Sinchula (1865):

Bhutan ceded the Bengal and Assam Dooars to the British
the region was incorporated into British India
forests were gradually cleared
tea plantations expanded across the foothill plains

This marked the beginning of the plantation economy of the Dooars.

Relationship to Modern Dooars Region
Today the term “Dooars” refers to the entire foothill belt south of Bhutan, particularly across:

Alipurduar district
Jalpaiguri district
parts of Darjeeling district

Many modern settlements and tea estates developed in areas historically associated with these frontier gateways.

Research Note
The precise geographical correspondence between some historical Dooar passes and modern locations remains uncertain.
Further investigation using:

colonial frontier maps
district gazetteers
British survey records

may clarify the exact locations of certain passes.

10_Concepts/Frontier-System/Eighteen_Doo.title: Eighteen_Dooars_(Frontier_Passes).md
10_Concepts/Frontier-System/Eighteen_Doo.content:
Eighteen Dooars (Athara Duars)
Meaning of the Term
The word Dooar (Duar) comes from the Hindustani word “Dwar”, meaning door or gateway.
Historically, the term referred to the mountain passes connecting the plains of Bengal and Assam with the hills of Bhutan. These passes served as the main routes for trade, administration, and military movement between Bhutan and the plains.
Over time, the name Dooars came to refer not only to the passes themselves but also to the fertile foothill region south of Bhutan where these gateways opened.
Related:
Dooars_Geography_Identity

Historical Context
Before British annexation in 1865, the Dooars region formed part of the frontier territory controlled by Bhutan.
Each Dooar was administered by a Bhutanese official known as a Jungpen.
The Jungpen controlled:

the mountain pass
surrounding foothill territory
collection of tribute from plains territories
local administration and frontier security

Because of this system, the Dooars functioned as strategic frontier gateways between Bhutan and the plains of Bengal and Assam.
Related:
Dooars_Frontier_Pre_1865

Bengal Dooars (Western Dooars)
These Dooars were located between the Teesta River and the Sankosh River, covering the frontier of Bengal and Cooch Behar.

Dalimkote
Mynaguri
Chamurchi
Luckee
Buxa
Bhulka
Bara
Goomar
Beepo
Cherrung
Bagk (Chota Bijnee)

These passes connected Bhutan with the plains of Cooch_Behar and northern Bengal.

Assam Dooars (Eastern Dooars)
These were located between the Sankosh River and the Dhansiri River, forming the Bhutan–Assam frontier.

Booree Goomah
Bailing
Shurkolla
Banska
Chappagoorie
Chapkahama
Bijnee

These Dooars connected Bhutan with the plains of Assam.

Strategic Importance
The Dooars passes were historically important because they:

served as trade routes between Bhutan and the Indian plains
allowed movement of goods such as salt, wool, and agricultural products
acted as military corridors during frontier conflicts
enabled Bhutan to maintain political influence over frontier territories


British Annexation
The strategic importance of the Dooars eventually led to conflict between Bhutan and British India.
During the Dooar_War_1864, British forces captured many of the frontier passes.
Following the war, the 1865_Treaty_of_Sinchula resulted in the annexation of the Dooars by British India.
After annexation, the region was gradually transformed through:

tea plantation expansion
forest exploitation
colonial settlement

10_Concepts/Human_Geography/Frontier_Soc.content:
Frontier Society in the Dooars
Before British annexation in 1865, the Dooars formed a frontier region controlled by Bhutan.
The region was administered through a system of frontier passes known as the Duars.
Bhutanese officials known as Jungpens controlled the passes and collected tribute from the plains.
Settlement remained limited due to:

dense forests
malaria
difficult terrain.
